text: SOURCE: QUALITY 2.0 DIFFICULTY 4.0 COS510 Jul 5th, 2017 For Credit: Yes Attendance: Not Mandatory Would Take Again: No Textbook: No very hard lot of homework QUALITY 5.0 DIFFICULTY 4.0 COS510 May 17th, 2015 For Credit: Yes Textbook: Yes The material is super interesting, but the class is pretty tough. Notes: - Multiple reviewers describe Appel's classes as rigorous and detail-oriented. - Students report dense material and a heavy workload; many recommend reading carefully and asking questions early.

Andrew Appel's teaching style is rigorous and detail-oriented. Students describe his lectures as thorough and sometimes dense, with a strong focus on correctness and systems-level reasoning. The course is known for its heavy workload, and many reviewers say it requires a lot of time outside class. Homework and exams are difficult but instructive. Several reviewers mentioned that the assignments are challenging and that grading is strict but fair. The exams test understanding of the concepts rather than rote memorization. Students who take Appel's classes should expect to read carefully, work consistently, and ask questions early. The course is often described as demanding, but the professors are seen as knowledgeable and helpful when students are prepared. Several reviewers mentioned projects that required debugging real systems code, which they found both exciting and time-consuming. The class also includes frequent quizzes and short in-class assessments, so staying current with the material is important. The final exam was described as fair but comprehensive, testing a broad range of topics from lectures and homework. Many students advised future takers to use previous assignments as study guides and to focus on understanding fundamentals rather than memorizing syntax.

SOURCE: QUALITY 3.5 DIFFICULTY 2.0 COS126 May 30th, 2015 For Credit: Yes Textbook: No Doug Clark is a good lecturer, but isn't too important to the COS 126 class. Whereas precepts are critical, I found lectures to be fairly skippable. Notes: - Reviewers describe Clark as a competent lecturer; many emphasize the relative importance of precepts over lectures in this course.

SOURCE: QUALITY 3.5 DIFFICULTY 4.0 CS131 Dec 25th, 2014 For Credit: Yes Textbook: Yes Great class. She makes lectures and references to the textbook clear. Only problem is that it is rather hand-wavy at times but perhaps this is understandable, since it is an introductory class. Good introduction to computer vision. QUALITY 4.0 DIFFICULTY 4.0 COS429 Aug 16th, 2008 Textbook: Yes Great class Notes: - Reviewers praise Fei-Fei Li for clear lectures and strong introductions to complex topics. - Some comments note that parts are hand-wavy or less formal, which can be acceptable for introductory material.

Brian Kernighan is an excellent lecturer, but his class is intense. His lectures move quickly and expect students to follow the logic without expecting too much hand-holding. Many students said his teaching was clear if you read the material before class, but the pace can be overwhelming if you rely only on lecture notes. Homework in his course is challenging and often graded strictly. Several reviewers mentioned that homework problems are conceptually deep and not always directly covered in lecture, so consistent practice is important. Exams are described as technically demanding and focused on problem-solving rather than memorization. Overall, students recommend attending every lecture and starting assignments early. One reviewer said the course is rewarding for students who enjoy rigorous programming and algorithms, but it can feel like a lot of work if you are not prepared for the pace. The professor is also praised for being approachable after class, and many students appreciated having a clear set of expectations for the final project. Some criticisms centered on the lack of review sessions, with students wishing there were more sample problems available before exams. A few reviewers explicitly warned future students not to underestimate the course. They recommended forming study

and many students appreciated having a clear set of expectations for the final project. Some criticisms centered on the lack of review sessions, with students wishing there were more sample problems available before exams. A few reviewers explicitly warned future students not to underestimate the course. They recommended forming study groups and reviewing lecture material daily to stay on top of the content.

Kai Li's lectures are highly structured and content-rich. Many students report that her classes move at a steady pace, with a strong focus on research examples and real-world applications. While the lectures are clear, the material can be dense, so students are encouraged to review notes immediately after class. The homework is demanding and often requires applying theory to new problems. Reviewers say that the assignments are useful for learning the material, but they can be time-consuming, especially when the problems require careful reasoning. Students generally find Kai Li accessible, but some noted that office hours are limited and appointments fill quickly. Overall, the course is rewarding for those who enjoy combining theory and application, but it requires disciplined study habits. Exam questions were described as fair but broad, incorporating both conceptual understanding and problem-solving. A few reviewers recommended practicing with past problem sets and seeking help early if a concept is unclear.

SOURCE: MATH101 Jun 10th, 2009 Textbook: Yes Excellent QUALITY 5.0 DIFFICULTY 1.0 COS522 Oct 5th, 2005 QUALITY 3.0 DIFFICULTY 5.0 No Comments Other dated reviews include a mix of high and low ratings reflecting different courses. Notes: - Ratings across years show variance; include specific course context when using these reviews.

Kevin Wayne's classes are organized and lecture-heavy. Reviewers consistently describe his lectures as clear but fast, with a strong emphasis on proofs and algorithmic thinking. He communicates the big ideas well, but students should expect to review proofs multiple times outside of class. The homework sets are considered difficult and time-consuming. Students say that the problem sets are proof-oriented and require careful writing. Some reviewers also note that the exams are challenging and require a strong understanding of the material rather than simple computation. For students who enjoy theory, Wayne's course is rewarding, but those who are weaker in proofs may find the workload steep. Office hours were described as helpful, though they can fill up quickly before exams. Some reviewers said the textbook is useful but not sufficient by itself; they needed lecture notes and external practice problems to fully understand the material. Grades were said to be reflective of effort, and missing problem set deadlines could significantly lower a student's grade. In addition, several students noted that collaboration was encouraged on homework when it was done properly, but clarity around what counts as collaboration could have been better.
